cards.title: Thinking Machines Releases Inkling: ~1T-Parameter Multimodal MoE with 1M Context
cards.why_it_matters: A flagship open-weight release from a notable new lab—975B total / 41B active parameters with native image, audio, and text input and 1M context—is a major event the whole field will discuss, especially given its novel architectural choices.
cards.summary: Inkling is a decoder-only MoE model with 975B total and 41B active parameters, trained on 45 trillion tokens of text, images, audio, and video. It uses relative attention (not RoPE), hybrid global/sliding-window attention (5:1 ratio), short 1D convolutions over hidden states, and 256 routed experts plus 2 always-active shared experts. Modality towers are lightweight hierarchical MLP patchifiers. Ships in BF16 and NVFP4, with speculative MTP layers and day-0 support in transformers, SGLang, and llama.cpp.

cards.title: Real World VoiceEQ: Human-Rated Benchmark Across 40+ Voice Models on 60+ Metrics
cards.why_it_matters: A large-scale, human-preference voice benchmark (1M+ ratings) covering ASR, TTS, and speech-to-speech is a useful calibration tool for anyone building or evaluating voice AI systems.
cards.summary: Real World VoiceEQ evaluates 40+ proprietary and open-source voice models across 15+ dimensions and 60+ metrics, grounded in over 1 million human ratings (785,000 TTS, 48,000 speech-to-speech). Key finding: no TTS system ranked in the top five across all eight capability groups, and speech-to-speech models showed the widest performance variation. Some systems were transcript-driven, ignoring paralinguistic cues like tone, pacing, and hesitation that humans use to infer confidence and emotion.

cards.title: MOJO: Semi-Supervised Spike-Tokenizing Framework Improves Neural Decoding with Unlabeled Data
cards.why_it_matters: For researchers working on BCI or neuro-foundation models, MOJO offers a practical SSL recipe that improves decoding under label scarcity—a pervasive real-world constraint in neural data collection.
cards.summary: MOJO combines masked autoencoding (SSL) with supervised objectives for spike-tokenizing neural decoders. Evaluated on monkey motor cortex reaching tasks and multi-regional mouse recordings (vision and decision-making), it outperforms purely supervised baselines, with gains most pronounced in few-shot finetuning scenarios. SSL also improves brain region classification and spike-statistics prediction without explicit optimization. MOJO extends to human electrocorticography during speech, reaching performance comparable to neuro-foundation models designed for continuous signals.

cards.title: Shor's Algorithm Formalized in Lean via Agentic AI, Including RSA-2048 and P-256 Resource Estimates
cards.why_it_matters: Machine-checked quantum cryptanalysis of RSA-2048 and P-256 via LLM-assisted Lean formalization is both a frontier result in formal verification and a concrete signal for post-quantum security timelines.
cards.summary: Software agents analyzed sources, wrote Lean code, and repaired proofs—with human review of scientific claims and machine-checking of formal proofs—to formalize Shor's algorithm and variants. The formalization covers quantum order-finding, reversible circuits for modular and elliptic-curve arithmetic, and logical resource estimates for breaking RSA-2048 (based on Quantum 5, 433) and P-256 (based on ASIACRYPT 2017), plus additional classical operation estimates.

cards.title: How Skylight Built Shippy: A Reliable AI Agent for Maritime Domain Awareness
cards.why_it_matters: A detailed, practitioner-level case study on production agent design—soul/skills/config decomposition, deterministic tool design, and auditability—directly applicable to any agentic system you might build or evaluate.
cards.summary: Shippy is Skylight's maritime AI agent built on Claude Opus 4.6 via the OpenClaw framework. Its architecture splits into soul (system prompt with explicit behavioral limits), skills (markdown spec files for API queries, EEZ/MPA lookups, vessel track interpretation, and map link generation), and config (swappable at runtime). Key reliability principles: tools are deterministic even when the model is not; the agent refuses legal determinations and speculation beyond data; all answers include source attribution and deep map links for human verification.

cards.title: Model Routing Is a Systems Optimization Problem: Lessons from AppWorld Benchmarking
cards.why_it_matters: Concrete empirical data on how caching, infrastructure, and multi-objective tradeoffs break naive model-routing assumptions—directly relevant if you're building or evaluating agentic pipelines with multiple LLM backends.
cards.summary: Across 417 AppWorld tasks with a CodeAct agent, Claude Sonnet 4.6 cost $79 total ($0.19/task) vs. GPT-4.1 at $155 ($0.37/task), despite GPT-4.1 having lower per-token sticker prices. The gap traced to cache-read pricing: agent workloads reuse context heavily, so Sonnet's lower cache-read cost dominated. The post also argues task difficulty is opaque at routing time and that latency depends more on serving infrastructure than model size, motivating treating routing as a multi-objective optimization rather than classification.

cards.title: CPM-Based Quantum LDPC Codes via Pair-Partition Constructions Reach Rate 0.44
cards.why_it_matters: New explicit quantum LDPC code constructions with concrete parameters are useful reference points for anyone tracking quantum error correction progress toward fault-tolerant systems.
cards.summary: The paper constructs CSS quantum LDPC codes from circulant permutation matrices (CPMs) parameterized by column weight J, row weight L, and prime lift size P. Key finite examples: a [[372,130,16]] code at rate 0.349 and a [[518,228,16]] code at rate 0.440, both (J,L)=(4,12) and (4,14)-regular girth-six respectively. Also reports [[472,122,14]] and [[488,126,14]] at (J,L)=(3,8). Distances verified by exhaustive low-weight exclusion plus explicit non-stabilizer witnesses.

cards.title: Trigonometric CV Gates Benchmarked on Trapped-Ion Hardware for Bosonic Simulation
cards.why_it_matters: Experimental gate-level characterization of a new CV primitive on real trapped-ion hardware is a concrete step toward fault-tolerant bosonic simulation—relevant for those tracking quantum hardware progress.
cards.summary: The paper experimentally demonstrates and benchmarks cosine gates on the QSCOUT trapped-ion platform using 3- and 4-ion Yb-171 chains. One-qumode cosine gates and two-qumode mode-resolved marginal benchmarks are characterized via Fock-space transition probabilities as a function of gate parameters and Trotter step count. Results are compared against simulations incorporating thermal initialization and motional dephasing, establishing these trigonometric CV gates as reusable primitives for bosonic Hamiltonian simulation and hybrid quantum algorithms.
